For AI initiatives specifically, the data readiness challenge is even more acute. AI models are only as good as the data they learn from. Clean, comprehensive, and well-governed master data across SAP and non-SAP systems isn't just nice to have it's the difference between AI that transforms your business and AI that disappoints.

The question for SAP Architects is simple: are you ready to make master data readiness the strategic priority it needs to be for both your S/4HANA transformation and your AI journey?

The organisations that answer "yes" and act decisively will be the ones leading their industries with AI-powered innovation built on a foundation of trusted data. The others will be left wondering why their expensive AI investments failed to deliver and the answer will be staring them in the face: they skipped the foundation.

 

The window of competitive advantage is closing. Organisations that built their data foundation correctly are already deploying AI at scale improving customer experiences, optimising operations, and making better decisions faster. Those still struggling with basic data quality aren't just behind on AI adoption they're watching the gap widen every quarter.

AI doesn't wait for you to fix your data. Your competitors who prioritised data quality are already leveraging AI to eat your market share.

The choice is stark: invest in master data readiness now and unlock AI's potential or continue treating data quality as "someone else's problem" and watch your AI initiatives fail one by one.

SAP is Here to Help

Recognising that master data readiness is a critical challenge for organisations embarking on S/4HANA and AI journeys, SAP offers dedicated services designed to guide you through this transformation:

SAP Data Readiness Discovery Workshop

SAP Business Data Cloud Discovery Workshop

These workshops provide the expertise, frameworks, and actionable insights to accelerate your master data journey. SAP's experienced Architects work alongside your teams to assess your unique challenges, design practical solutions, and establish the data foundation that both S/4HANA and AI success demand.

You don't have to navigate the complexity of master data readiness alone. SAP is here to help and advice customers on building the data foundation that unlocks the full potential of digital transformation and AI innovation.

Ready to begin your master data readiness journey? Reach out to your SAP account team to learn more about these discovery workshops and how SAP can support your organisation's path to data excellence.

entry.title: Company Code Merge Using DMLT Business Transformation Service
entry.content:
(The Power of One: Streamlining Success through DMLT )

Company Code Merge using DMLT Business Transformation Service : A Comprehensive Guide

Introduction It is noted that company code mergers are an essential component of enterprise transformation, often necessitated by legal compliance, restructuring, or mergers and acquisitions. Financial reporting is simplified through this consolidation, and DMLT Business Transformation services are utilized to streamline the planning, data migration, and support phases.

What is a Company Code Merge? A company code merge is defined as the process by which two or more legal entities are combined into a single unified company code within the system. Financial and master data are transferred into the new entity, while the legacy company codes are subsequently decommissioned,.

When to Use SAP Company Code Merge

  • Legal Mergers and Acquisitions: It is applied when legal entities merge or are acquired, ensuring the system reflects the new organizational structure.
  • Internal Restructuring: It is utilized to combine subsidiaries or divisions, achieving economies of scale and simplified management.
  • Tax or Regulatory Compliance: It is executed to consolidate unneeded company codes while retaining historical data for regulatory requirements.
  • Consolidation of Systems: It is performed when multiple legacy or departmental systems are centralized to reduce IT complexity.
  • Streamlining Financial Reporting: It is implemented to merge smaller legal entities, thereby simplifying the production of consolidated financial statements.

 

Benefits of SAP Company Code Merge

  • Streamlined Financial Reporting: The generation of reports is simplified, as all financial data is consolidated into a single entity.
  • Cost Savings and Efficiency: IT overhead and infrastructure costs are reduced by leveraging a unified operational system.
  • Improved Data Accuracy and Integrity: Data quality is enhanced, and the risk of duplicate records is minimized through centralized master data maintenance.
  • Enhanced Operational Efficiency: Business processes, such as procurement and sales, are standardized and processed more efficiently across the organization.
  • Reduced IT Complexity: System landscapes are simplified, leading to easier maintenance, fewer integration issues, and faster upgrades.
  • Compliance and Governance: Audit processes and regulatory filings are streamlined by utilizing a unified accounting framework.
  • Strategic Agility: Real-time, comprehensive data access is provided to management, enabling faster strategic decisions.

Process of Merging Company Codes

  • Step 1: Pre-Planning and Analysis
    • Scope Definition: The target company code, required data transfers, and project timelines are identified by relevant stakeholders.
    • Impact Analysis: The operational effects on financial statements, IT systems, and compliance are evaluated to formulate mitigation strategies.
    • Gap Analysis: Discrepancies in system configuration and master data between source and target codes are identified for resolution.
    • Project Plan and Roadmap: Detailed tasks, timelines, and team responsibilities are established across all project phases.
    • Change Management: Organizational impacts are addressed through targeted communication and user training preparations.
  • Step 2: Clean-Up and Prepare Data
    • Finalize Go-Live: A cut-off date is established, after which no new transactions are permitted in the source systems.
    • Clear Open Items: Outstanding invoices and unclarified items are settled to ensure accurate balance sheet transfers.
    • Reconcile Intercompany Transactions: Financial balances between the merging entities are settled to prevent double-counting.
    • Archive or Clean-Up Old Data: Historical data is archived or deleted according to retention requirements, often supported by DMLT tools.
    • Data Quality Checks: The completeness and accuracy of all master and transaction data are verified prior to the migration.
  • Step 3: Configure the Target Company Code
    • Align Configuration: Financial settings, such as charts of accounts and currencies, are aligned with the source codes.
    • Replicate Master Data: Master records are created or mapped in the target system to match the source data parameters.
    • Assign Organizational Units: Relevant organizational structures, such as plants or sales areas, are properly assigned to the target code.
    • Transfer Customization: Custom configurations and system settings are migrated and updated to ensure target compatibility.
    • Update Interfaces and Integrations: External system interfaces are redirected to communicate with the new legal entity.
    • Transformations Requirements: Additional technical transformations, such as the merging of controlling areas, are completed if necessary.
  • Step 4: Perform the Data Transformation
    • Migration/Transformation Approach finalization: The transformation approach is decided, potentially including on-the-fly transformations utilizing DMLT services.
    • Extract Data from Source Systems: All relevant financial and master data is extracted as of the established cut-off date.
    • Transform and Validate Data: Data is mapped to the target format and thoroughly validated against the source totals.
    • Load Data into Target System: The transformed data is imported into the target system using batch jobs or specialized DMLT tools.
    • Post Migration Adjustments: Outstanding entries are posted and missing master records are corrected via system adjustments.
  • Step 5: Testing and Validation
    • Reconciliation Testing: Migrated balances and operational transactions are comprehensively reconciled with the source data.
    • Functional Testing: Key business processes are tested to confirm that expected functionalities operate smoothly.
    • System Integration Testing: Data flow and interactions with all integrated external systems are verified.
    • User Acceptance Testing (UAT): Daily operational tasks are executed by end-users to confirm system readiness.
    • Performance Testing: System capacity is evaluated to ensure high transaction volumes can be handled post-merge.
    • Compliance and Audit Checks: The accuracy of financial records and the existence of auditable migration trails are validated.
  • Step 6: Final Cutover and Rollout
    • Freeze the Source Systems: Source systems are locked against new transactions following successful testing phases.
    • Perform the Cutover: Ongoing business operations are redirected entirely to the unified target system.
    • Unfreeze the Target System: Posting periods are opened in the target system to allow for normal operational transactions.
    • Communicate the Change: Internal stakeholders and users are formally informed of the successful system transition.
  • Step 7: Post-Implementation Support
    • Monitoring and Support: Immediate post-merge support is deployed to resolve any arising operational issues.
    • Data Archiving: Archiving of legacy data from the old company codes is conducted using appropriate tools.
    • Decommission the Source Company Codes: Old company codes are fully deactivated in accordance with recommended system procedures.
    • Review and Continuous Improvement: A post-implementation review is conducted to document lessons learned and refine future processes.

SAP S/4HANA and Data Model Changes

  • Universal Journal (ACDOCA): It is required that all historical transactions from legacy codes be loaded into the unified journal structure.
  • Single Business Partner: Customer and vendor records are mapped and consolidated into a unified Business Partner model.
  • Document Splitting: Target configurations are adjusted to ensure financial transactions are split according to new reporting requirements.
  • System Landscape: DMLT services and conversion tools are utilized to ensure a cinsitent and reliable transformation. 
  • Post-Merger Configuration: New consolidation setups and reporting views are established for the merged financial entity.

Key Considerations and Challenges

  • Legal and Regulatory Compliance: Adherence to standard accounting principles and data protection laws must be ensured.
  • Data Integrity and Accuracy: Thorough validation is required to prevent incorrect reporting resulting from migration errors.
  • Master Data Harmonization: Overlapping customer and vendor records must be resolved to create a consistent set of master data.
  • System and Process Impact: Affected business processes and external interfaces must be identified and integrated seamlessly.
  • Downtime and Business Continuity: Operational disruption must be minimized through coordinated cutover strategies and phased rollouts.
  • Organizational Change Management: User adoption is improved by executing comprehensive communication and training plans.
  • Technical Challenges: Complexities regarding custom code, high data volumes, and overall system performance must be addressed.
  • Post-Merger Adjustments: Strategic plans must be established to handle reporting differences and opening balance adjustments.
  • Audit and Compliance: Process transparency must be maintained to ensure all data transfers satisfy external audit requirements.
  • Cost and Resource Management: Project budgets, timelines, and technical resources must be carefully managed to prevent operational delays.

Conclusion

  • Strategic Alignment: The transformation is planned in consultation with key business units to align with organizational goals.
  • Thorough Planning: Scopes and impacts are detailed early in the process to effectively manage expectations.
  • Data Management: Data accuracy and integrity are maintained through the use of DMLT   Solutions.
  • Change Management: Smooth system adoption is achieved by involving stakeholders and addressing concerns early.
  • Use of Tools: High-quality migration outcomes are secured by leveraging specialized DMLT tools.
  • Continuous Improvement: Ongoing system optimization is fostered by conducting detailed post-implementation reviews.

entry.title: Creating a DQM Validation Rule for Business Partner Role Assignment in SAP MDG
entry.content:
Overview

Data quality is critical in Master Data Governance (MDG), especially when dealing with Business Partners. One common data quality issue occurs when Business Partners are created without any Business Partner Role assigned. Since roles determine how a Business Partner participates in business processes (customer, supplier, etc.), missing roles can break downstream applications.

The goal is to create a DQM validation rule in SAP Master Data Governance that prevents the creation or modification of Business Partners without role assignments. The system will show a validation error or warning as shown in the following screenshot:

The validation logic works as follows:

  1. The rule iterates over Business Partner records in table BUT000.
  2. For each Business Partner ID, a table operation checks table BUT100.
  3. If no entry exists in BUT100 for that BP, the validation rule raises an error.

This ensures that every Business Partner has at least one role assigned before it can be saved.

Prerequisites

Before implementing the rule, ensure the following requirements are met:

  • SAP Master Data Governance with Data Quality Management (DQM) enabled
  • User authorization with the SAP_BR_BUPA_MASTER_STEWARD role or equivalent
  • Access to the SAP Fiori application Validation Rules for Business Partners (App IDs F4084 or F4082A (newer releases)

Rule Configuration

Step 1 — Access the DQM Validation App

  1. Open the SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Launch the Validation Rules for Business Partners app.

This UI/App allows you to manage and create validation rules for Business Partner data

Step 2 — Create a New Validation Rule

Click Create and enter the following information:

Field

Value

Rule ID

CHECK_BP_ROLE_MANDATORY

Rule Name

BP Role Assignment Required

Base Table

BUT000 (Business Partner General Data)

Checked Field

BUT0BK-PARTNER_ROLE (optional but recommended)

The base table must be BUT000, even though the validation checks data stored in another table

Step 3 — Maintain General Information

Fill in the business metadata for the rule:

Description
Ensures every Business Partner has at least one role assigned.

Reason
Business Partners without roles cannot participate in business processes.

Rule Owner
Data Steward responsible for Business Partner data.

Implementation Expert
Technical contact responsible for BRF+ implementation.

Save the rule by clicking Create.

Step 4 — Technical Implementation

Configure Rule Usage

  1. In the Usage section, select Add.
  2. Choose Check in Central Governance.
  3. Click Prepare to generate the BRF+ application.
  4. Select Send for Implementation.

This enables the rule logic to be edited.

Scope Expression Configuration

The Scope Expression defines which Business Partners should be validated.

Steps:

  1. Open the Scope Expression link.
  2. The Simplified BRF+ Workbench appears.
  3. Define the scope.

Example scope:

Partner Category = 2 (Organization)

This means the validation rule will only apply to organizational Business Partners.

You can also configure the rule to apply to all Business Partners.

Condition Expression Implementation

The main validation logic is defined in the Condition Expression.

Steps:

  1. Open the Condition Expression.
  2. Remove the dummy condition.
  3. Create a new expression.

Choose:

Expression Type: Table Operation

Example name: BUT100_CHECK

Table Operation Configuration

Configure the operation as follows:

Parameter

Value

Operation

Has at least

Table

Procedure Call

Procedure

BUT0BK lookup ALL

Condition

Partner Role is not initial

This configuration checks if at least one role exists for the Business Partner.

 

Although the rule loops over BUT000, the lookup ultimately checks role entries in BUT100.

This cross-table validation is the key technical detail of this solution.

Activation and Deployment

Once testing is complete:

  1. Select Send for Testing to change the rule status.
  2. After validation, choose Approve.
  3. Go to the Usage tab.
  4. Click Enable for Check in Central Governance.

The rule is now active and will validate Business Partner data during governance processes.

Testing the Rule

Test the validation logic.

Test Scenarios

Scenario

Expected Result

BP without roles

Validation error

BP with one role

Validation passes

BP with multiple roles

Validation passes

Testing ensures that only valid Business Partner records can be saved.

 

Troubleshooting

Rule Not Triggering

Check the following:

  • All BRF+ expressions are active
  • Rule status is Approved
  • Usage is Enabled

Performance Issues

If validation becomes slow:

  • Restrict the Scope Expression
  • Limit evaluation to specific Business Partner categories
  • Avoid unnecessary table operations

 

Best Regards

Steffen

entry.title: Parallel Class Assignment on Functional Location
entry.content:
1. Issue / Problem Statement

It has been observed that the technical object classification values currently do not meet the expected data quality standards in terms of

  • Completeness
  • Consistency
  • Accuracy
  • Uniqueness

Due to the large volume of legacy data and inconsistencies across multiple sources, the business has not been able to complete the required data cleansing activities within the planned timeline during the S4 HANA transformation.

At the same time, the project team does not want to lose the existing legacy classification data, as it may still provide valuable reference information for historical context, engineering analysis, or future asset validation.

Therefore, there is a need for a mechanism to retain the legacy data without impacting the operational master data quality of the new S4 system.

2. Proposed Solution

Parallel Class Assignment on Functional Location in SAP

To address the above challenge, the proposed solution is to introduce a parallel classification structure on Functional Locations (FLOC) in SAP.

This approach will allow the following:

  • Migration of existing legacy Technical Object Classification values into a separate reference class
  • Preservation of historical data without interfering with the new standardized classification model
  • Future reference and traceability for engineering and maintenance teams

Key Concept

Two classification layers will exist for the same Functional Location:

Classification Type

Purpose

Standard SAP FLOC Class

Clean, validated classification used for operational processes

Reference Class (Parallel Class)

Legacy data stored for historical reference

This ensures:

  • Clean operational data
  • No loss of legacy information
  • Reduced dependency on immediate data cleansing

3. Benefits of the Approach

✔ Preserves legacy technical data
✔ Enables progressive data cleansing over time
✔ Maintains clean operational classification for SAP processes
✔ Supports future engineering analysis and traceability
✔ Avoids project delays caused by data quality remediation

4. Implementation Considerations

  • Create a separate class type for reference data (if required)
  • Define parallel class assignment to functional location.
  • Migrate TOC values into reference characteristics
  • Restrict usage to display/reference only
  • Ensure governance rules to prevent operational usage

5. Project Impact

Area

Impact

Go-Live

Not go-live critical

Scope

Implemented via the scope management process

Business Impact

Minimal operational impact

Data Strategy

Enables controlled transition to clean master data

Functional Location with Standard Class “003” assignment:

Functional Location with “Z03” Class assignment:

SAP Configuration of “Z03” Class for Functional Location:

SAP Configuration of “Z03” Class for Functional Location:

“Z03” Class Organizational Areas Assignment:

Class Type "Z03" Class A11 Organizational Areas G, R assigned:

In Characteristic A0957 Class type “Z03” assignment in Restrictions:

Key Takeaways

In many asset-intensive transformations, legacy technical data rarely meets the required data quality standards at the time of system migration. Rather than losing valuable historical information, a parallel class assignment on functional location (FLOC) in SAP offers a pragmatic solution. This approach enables organizations to preserve legacy technical object classification data as reference while maintaining clean, standardized master data for operational processes. It balances data governance with business practicality, allowing progressive data cleansing without delaying project timelines. A simple yet powerful strategy to protect legacy knowledge while building a future-ready asset data foundation in SAP EAM.

#SAP #SAPAssetManagement #EAM #MasterData #DataGovernance #DigitalTransformation

entry.title: Governance: The Word Everyone Uses and Nobody Agrees On
entry.content:
We're in a strange moment for governance.

The EU AI Act is moving into enforcement. Enterprises are racing to deploy generative AI while their compliance teams scramble to figure out what "responsible use" even means in practice.

And in the middle of all this, everyone keeps using the word "governance" as if we all agree on what it means.

We don't.

If I put your data team, your AI team, and your SAP team in a room and asked them all to define "governance," I'd get at least three different answers. Your data team would talk about quality, lineage, and stewardship. Your AI team would talk about fairness, model risk, and responsible use. Your SAP team would talk about master data workflows, golden records, and change approvals.

They're all right. And they're all incomplete.

Governance isn't a single discipline. It's a spectrum. And organizations that treat it as one fragmented program after another, each with its own framework, committee, and slide deck, keep reinventing the wheel.

Here's the position I want to stake out: the governance problem isn't a capability gap. It's a visibility gap. Most enterprises already have 70% of what they need. They just can't see how the pieces connect.

Data Governance and AI Governance: Two Questions, One Continuum

Data governance answers a foundational question: can we trust our data?

That means defining ownership, measuring quality, controlling access, and tracking how data flows across systems. Most mature organizations have the building blocks: stewards, quality rules, audit trails, access controls.

AI governance picks up where data governance stops, but asks a different question: can we trust what the system does with the data?

This is the shift from managing information to managing decisions. AI governance introduces concerns that data governance was never designed to handle: bias testing, human-in-the-loop design, drift monitoring, and output validation. The accountability surface grows because the impact surface grows.

But here's what most organizations get wrong: they treat these as separate programs. The smarter approach is to recognize that most data governance capabilities are already AI governance capabilities, applied to a different lifecycle stage.

Data quality management builds trust in model performance. Lineage tracking helps surface bias. Access controls define boundaries for ethical use. Auditability enables defensible decision-making.

Same muscles. Different emphasis: inputs versus outputs.

SAP MDG: The Operational Governance Layer Most AI Discussions Miss

This is where the conversation gets practical, and where I want to challenge a blind spot.

SAP Master Data Governance is the operational execution layer. It's where governance policies become enforceable processes, embedded in the workflows your business runs on every day.

When someone wants to create a new vendor record, MDG defines who requests it, who reviews it, what quality checks it passes through, and who approves it before it reaches your production system. After a merger, MDG provides the framework to match, merge, and harmonize records at scale. These aren't administrative niceties. They're governance you can touch.

And here's the connection most people miss: SAP MDG is foundational AI governance that nobody labels as such.

A duplicate vendor record isn't just an ERP nuisance. It propagates into your analytics layer, your spend analysis models, and your AI-driven procurement recommendations. A miscategorized material doesn't just break reporting. It trains your demand forecasting model on flawed assumptions. When MDG enforces a validation rule or rejects a poorly formed record, it is protecting the integrity of the data that AI systems will eventually consume.

This is governance work happening before any model gets involved.

I've seen this disconnect firsthand. A company spends years implementing MDG with robust workflows, quality rules, and stewardship models. Then a separate team launches an AI governance initiative from a blank whiteboard, completely unaware that the operational backbone they need is already running. The disconnect is organizational, not technical. Bridging it is one of the highest-value, lowest-cost improvements most SAP customers can make.

Where SAP Business Data Cloud Closes the Loop

SAP Business Data Cloud takes this further. By exposing SAP's business data as semantically rich, governed, AI-ready data products, it creates the layer where master data quality, business context, and AI consumption connect in one governed structure.

The governance lineage becomes continuous: MDG enforces quality at the source; SAP BDC ensures that what reaches the AI layer is semantically meaningful, access-controlled, and auditable. The questions "is the record clean?" and "is the AI reasoning over trustworthy data?" finally have the same answer, because they share the same governed foundation.

SAP customers building toward enterprise AI don't need to start their governance story from scratch. The architecture is already pointing in the right direction.

The Bottom Line

Governance is elusive because we treat it as a collection of separate problems. Data governance asks whether the data is correct. AI governance asks whether the outcome is acceptable. Master data governance asks whether the record is clean and approved.

These aren't competing questions. They're complementary layers of the same capability: making trustworthy, accountable, defensible decisions at scale.

The organizations that get this right won't have the fanciest frameworks. They'll be the ones that see governance as a single connected spectrum, recognize that most of the pieces are already in place, and have the clarity to wire them together.

Governance isn't a framework problem. It's a visibility problem. And for most SAP customers, the infrastructure to solve it is closer than they think.

entry.title: The Day in the Life of a Data Steward – Meet Susan
entry.content:
When Joe, a quota-driven sales rep, opened his monthly dashboard, he felt a familiar knot in his stomach. His quota attainment looked wrong — revenue numbers didn’t match what he saw in the company’s customer relationship management (CRM) system; some closed orders seemed to vanish, and his commission estimate was off. He needed answers fast. Enter Susan, the Sales Data Steward. 

Susan’s phone pinged with Joe’s message: “Numbers don’t add up — can you help?” She smiled; this was exactly the kind of problem her role was designed to solve. Over coffee, she walked Joe through how she would restore trust in his sales metrics — and how he could rely on a single source of truth going forward. 

Accelerate data projects with data products 

Susan started with the first goal, to build a Sales Performance data product that everyone — sellers, managers, finance — can trust. She connected the primary sources: SAP S/4HANA sales orders, the CRM opportunity records Joe used daily, and the billing system where invoices were issued. SAP Business Data Cloud catalog harvested metadata from each source so Susan could see where definitions diverged. 

She profiled the datasets, spotting familiar issues: duplicate orders, inconsistent currencies, and missing booking dates. To fix this she defined clear business semantics — canonical definitions for “revenue,” “closed order,” and “booking date” — using the unified semantic layer in Business Data Cloud. She set quality rules (standardize currency, reconcile order-to-invoice, remove duplicates) and implemented transformations in SAP Datasphere, a component of Business Data Cloud, to apply fixes and compute KPIs like revenue recognition and quota attainment. 

Before publishing the derived Sales Performance data product, Susan documented every transformation and lineage step in the catalog. When Joe asked how they could trace any KPI back to a source row, she showed him the lineage diagram — from invoice record to summed revenue — so audits and investigations became straightforward instead of detective work. 

A unified, trusted view of your business 

Joe also needed a single view of his accounts. Sometimes an account in CRM had multiple IDs in S/4HANA or marketing systems; duplicates made it hard for him to track customer history. Susan prioritized a customer 360 derived product that linked the consolidated customer master with transactional data (orders, returns, interactions). 

Using SAP Master Data Governance, Susan checked out the data quality dashboards to ensure customer master was up to standards – cleansed and consolidated into golden records. In the Business Data Cloud semantic layer, she defined account hierarchies and segmentation, so Joe’s territory reports and account rollups would always use the same definitions.  In addition, to protect sales contacts and sensitive fields, Susan applied sensitivity labels and masking in the data catalog and enforced access policies for downstream tools. That meant Joe could see the data he needed for selling without exposing PII (Personally Identifiable Information) beyond what his role allowed. 

Ensuring data integrity and trust 

Joe’s commission complaint was the final piece. Compensation calculations and territory mappings had been a source of friction — organizational changes and inconsistent territory definitions across the CRM and human resource (HR) systems were creating mismatches. Susan treated territory and compensation as governed data products: she registered territory and compensation primary products (SAP SuccessFactors for rep assignments, CRM for territories, and the compensation system) and modeled territory and quota semantics in the semantic layer. 

She produced derived products that calculated compensation and applied quota rules, with explicit lineage and timestamped transformation steps. Role-based access and masking protected who could view compensation details. Retention and archival tags supported audit requirements, so if finance or internal auditors asked for the computation trail, Susan could provide it immediately. 

Delivering value and promoting self-service 

Once sales performance, customer 360, and compensation data products were published, Susan put them into the SAP Datasphere Data Marketplace with clear, plain-language descriptions, common queries, KPIs, and her contact details. She added usage examples and steward notes in the catalog, so business users like Joe could discover and use the data without guessing which dataset was trusted. 

Because access was governed and published via delta share, Joe’s analytics tools could connect directly and safely. Susan also continued to monitor the master data, with data quality scores (e.g., completeness, duplicates, currency consistency). When a quality alert fired, she had the ability to execute remediation flows with Master Data Governance. 

Watch a demo of ensuring trusted data products with SAP MDG and SAP BDC

Turn fragmented sources into trusted, accessible data  

A week later Joe refreshed his dashboard. The numbers aligned. Quota attainment matched expectations, commission calculations were transparent, and his account views reflected the single customer 360 view. He could see exactly which invoice contributed to his recognized revenue and who to contact if something looked off — Susan’s catalog entry and contact information were a click away. 

Susan didn’t just fix a dashboard — she changed how the sales organization accessed and trusted data. By registering primary data products, defining business semantics, applying quality rules, and publishing steward-curated data products with documented lineage and governed access, she turned fragmented sources into reliable, reusable data. Joe felt empowered with self-serve analytics while knowing there was stewardship behind the numbers. 

Conclusion 

If you’re starting as a Data Steward, Susan’s playbook maps directly to the operational checklist: prioritize high-value domains, leverage Master Data Governance for trusted master data,  use Business Data Cloud to harvest metadata, register and profile primary products, define semantics in the unified layer, enforce quality and masking, publish derived products to the marketplace, and monitor KPIs and usage. The result is faster time-to-value for business users, fewer shadow-analytics, and auditable, repeatable outcomes for the organization. 

Recommended Resources 

Blog Post: What is a Business Data Fabric? 

Blog Post: What are data products? 

Best Practice Article: The converged data imperative: How to feed AI, govern data, and secure the future 

Blog Post: Master data is a strategic, business-critical asset 

Hands-on: SAP Business Data Cloud basic trial

entry.title: SAP MDG News: AI Use Cases delivered, demoed and presented
entry.content:
Last week, I had the opportunity to present the latest SAP MDG and AI developments to a major client. Since the 2023 release, SAP MDG has unlocked numerous AI use cases, with most integrated directly into Joule and additional embedded AI features available. The live demo showcased all these capabilities in action.

Key AI Use Cases Overview

We're currently seeing three interaction types of Joule with MDG that are transforming how users work with master data:

Use Case 1: Informational

Knowledge at Your Fingertips: With Joule integrated into the SAP Launchpad, business users can ask MDG-related questions without leaving their current workspace. Instead of opening external browsers or applications, users can simply ask questions like "Does MDG support parallel processes for the same object?" Joule accesses the complete SAP Help documentation and SAP Community to deliver high-quality answers instantly.

Use Case 2: Navigational

Smart Data Discovery: Users can pose specific questions about master data within their MDG system. For example, asking "Show me all Business Partners with NWT in their name" displays results directly within the Joule chatbot. Users can then navigate directly from Joule to the appropriate app or action, eliminating the traditional process of opening the correct app, searching for business partners, and then proceeding with tasks.

 

Use Case 3: Transactional

Guided Process Creation: This powerful capability is already available in MDG cloud ready mode. When creating a new Business Partner via Joule, users interact within the chatbot interface, receiving guided assistance with the right questions for BP creation. The system also provides duplicate detection alerts directly within the chat experience

Custom AI Solutions Discussion

Beyond standard use cases, we explored customer-specific implementations using SAP Business AI to enhance master data quality. The concept of an "Attribute Recommendation Agent" could automatically improve data quality through regular evaluation and remediation processes, keeping master data current and accurate through automated quality checks.

Client Feedback Section

The feedback on delivered use cases was very positive! We also discussed exciting possibilities for implementing custom AI use cases with SAP Business AI to further enhance their master data governance processes.

Call to Action

For more detailed insights, check out this comprehensive blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/meet-joule-your-ai-copilot-for-sap-master-data-governance/ba-p/14273811

Meet Joule - Your AI Copilot for SAP Master Data Governance 🤖

Best regards,

Steffen
